text: This book reveals and examines the exercise of power in the field of global health and its outcomes. As such, Rethinking Global Health examines some of the most critical issues for global health today – as fundamental as the questions of ‘who’ global health is ‘for’ and ‘what’ obstructs the creation of adaptive responses to the complex health needs of communities, populations and individuals worldwide. Spanning topics from global mental health to Ebola disease, this text powerfully brings together power analyses, interviews and personal anecdotes gathered by the author over the course of ten years of work in the field of global health. It provides perspectives on how the pursuit of global approaches to improving health can so often result in what is unexpected, unexpected in terms of not only the geographical locations that are involved, but also the broader social and health dynamics that emerge. Dr Burgess introduces a new paradigm for Global Health, Transformative Global Health, that points to a need for global health researchers and practitioners to reconsider the principles that guide and shape their practice; principles that would be anchored to considerations of the interests of communities and populations whose health is the subject of focus of global activity, and where the interests of those communities are taken into account at every stage and in all aspects of the global interventions and activities that are created to address their needs. This book is key reading for anyone involved in global and public health studies and will also be relevant to activists, practitioners and advocates working within the global health and health equity fields internationally.
explanation: The passage reads like polished book-promotion copy with domain-specific claims; while well-structured, there are no clear AI artifacts or repetitive patterns, making human authorship plausible though AI assistance cannot be ruled out.
